steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: After the comma following ‘headrests,’ the phrase ‘their design ______ airflow …’ is separated from the main clause. The phrase after the comma is an absolute phrase built from a noun phrase plus a participle (‘their design prioritizing...’), adding detail to the main clause without creating a new finite clause. A finite verb (one with its own tense) would create a grammatically broken structure.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘was prioritizing’: A past progressive finite verb. This makes ‘their design was prioritizing …’ a second independent clause after the comma, which would require a semicolon or conjunction — not a comma alone. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘had prioritized’: A past perfect finite verb. Same problem as A — a finite verb creates an independent clause that cannot follow a comma without a conjunction. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘prioritizing’: A present participle. Combined with ‘their design,’ this forms an absolute phrase (‘their design prioritizing …’) that correctly modifies the main clause without creating a run-on. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘prioritized’: A simple past finite verb, producing another independent clause after the comma — the same error as A and B. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
